content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 16, 2017

Sarah E. Tobias
Attorney at Law
Govette & Associates, Inc.
1330 L Street Suite G
Fresno, CA 93721

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-112

Dear Ms. Tobias:

This letter responds to your two requests for advice on behalf of Tulare City
Councilmember Greg Nunley regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. Please note:

• We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and
any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

• We are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section
1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest.

• In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Tulare County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity as of the date this letter was issued. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).)

• We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is
not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See
Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Does Section 1091.1 allow Councilmember Nunley to subdivide lands he owns or has an
interest in?

2. May the Councilmember, as a private developer, properly enter into a subdivision
agreement with the City regarding lands he owns or has an interest in pursuant to that

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-112
Page No. 2

section?²

3. Assuming Councilmember Nunley may enter into subdivision agreements with the City
of Tulare, may these subdivision agreements contain reimbursement provisions?

4. Assuming Councilmember Nunley may enter into subdivision agreements containing
reimbursement provisions and assuming all of the conditions for reimbursement are properly
satisfied, may he be paid the appropriate reimbursements without creating a conflict of interest?

5. If Councilmember Nunley is not permitted to enter into any such subdivision or
reimbursement agreement with the City of Tulare, how should improvements, dedications, and
reimbursements pertaining to his land/developments be dealt with by the City?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Pursuant to Section 1091.1, Councilmember Nunley may subdivide property that he owns
or in which he has an ownership interest, so long as Councilmember Nunley abstains from any
participation on any matter pertaining to the subdivision, including the reimbursement agreement,
and fully discloses the nature of his interest in the land.

2. Section 1091.1 contemplates that a subdivision agreement between the City and the
Councilmember falls within the exception as discussed further below.

3. The City may enter into the agreement with the Councilmember and may reimburse him
pursuant to the reimbursement procedure set forth in Sections 66485-66489 so long as the
Councilmember abstains from any participation on any matter pertaining to the subdivision,
including the reimbursement agreement, and the Councilmember fully discloses the nature of his
interest in the land.

4. The Councilmember may be paid appropriate reimbursements.

5. Since we conclude that Councilmember Nunley is permitted to enter into the subdivision
and reimbursement agreement with the City of Tulare with respect to his land, we do not respond to
this last question.

FACTS

Financial Interests: Councilmember Nunley was elected to the Tulare City Council in the
November 8, 2016 election. Councilmember Nunley, in his private capacity, is also a business
owner, land owner, and property developer in the City of Tulare.

Councilmember Nunley currently lists the following business entities and general
descriptions of the businesses in his Statement of Economic Interests:

1. Great Valley Land Co., LLC (Real Estate- sales and rental income)

² You provided clarification on this question in a correspondence received on May 10, 2017.

File No. A-17-112
Page No. 3

2. Great Valley Land Company LLC (Real Estate- sales and rental income)

3. Del Lago Place LLC (Real Estate- sales income)

4. Great Valley Builders Inc. (Real Estate- construction and real estate sales income)

5. Swift Homes Inc. (Construction income)

6. Quest Equity LLC (Real Estate income)

7. Compares (Restaurant- food sales income)

8. La Tula Investments (Real Estate income)

9. Great Valley Builders Inc. (Real Property; row interest)

10. Quest Equity LLC (Real Property; offer on property)

Councilmember Nunley also lists the following interests in real property:

1. 30 Acres around 1969 Hillman and Corvina in Tulare, CA

2. 4 Acres near 1418 Bordolino in Tulare, CA

3. Tesori Subdivision in Tulare, CA

4. Quail Creek Subdivision in Tulare, CA

5. Parcel near 1878 Mooney Blvd. in Tulare, CA

6. Bella Oaks (6 lots) in Tulare, CA

Prior to being elected to the City Council, Mr. Nunley regularly entered into subdivision agreements with the City of Tulare while conducting business as a property developer and land owner. Both the Government Code and local ordinances of the City of Tulare require subdivisions to provide improvements as required by the Code and by the City's conditions of map approval. Part of the improvement process includes entering into subdivision agreements between the City and the subdivider. In situations of oversize improvements, the subdivision agreements may include reimbursement agreements between the City and the subdivider. When these agreements are entered into and the conditions for reimbursement satisfied, subdividers may be entitled to reimbursements from the City for oversize improvements.

File No. A-17-112
Page No. 4

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

We generally employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a conflict of
interest under Section 1090.³ However, you ask only about the application of step 5 and whether the
remote interest exception in Section 1091.1 applies.

Section 1091.1 provides:

"The prohibition against an interest in contracts provided by this article or
any other provision of law shall not be deemed to prohibit any public officer or
member of any public board or commission from subdividing lands owned by him
or in which he has an interest and which subdivision of lands is effected under the
provisions of Division 2 (commencing with Section 66410) of Title 7 of the
Government Code or any local ordinance concerning subdivisions; provided, that
(a) said officer or member of such board or commission shall first fully disclose
the nature of his interest in any such lands to the legislative body having
jurisdiction over the subdivision thereof, and (b) said officer or member of such
board or commission shall not cast his vote upon any matter or contract
concerning said subdivision in any manner whatsoever."

The Attorney General considered the scope of this exception in an opinion in 1998. In
Opinion No. 98-1001(81 Ops.Cal. Atty. Gen. 373), the Attorney General addressed whether a
county board of supervisors may enter into an agreement with the subdividers of a parcel of
property that would require the installation of a larger storm drain system than necessary for the
particular subdivision, with the county reimbursing the subdividers for the additional cost, if a
member of the board of supervisors has an ownership interest in the parcel. The Attorney General
concluded that the county board of supervisors could enter into such an agreement despite the facts
that a member of the board of supervisors had an ownership interest in the parcel. They reasoned:

The Subdivision Map Act (§§ 66410-66499.37; "Act") allows a city or
county, as a condition of granting subdivision map approval, to require a
subdivider to construct public improvements, such as streets and sewers, or
donate land or money for public facilities that will be needed due to the
subdivision and future use of the property. Not only may a subdivider be required
to construct improvements benefitting the proposed subdivision, he may also be
required to install improvements benefitting property not within the proposed
subdivision. (71 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 163, 163-164 (1988).)

* * *

³ These steps are: (1) is the official subject to the provisions of Section 1090; (2) does the decision at issue
involve a contract; (3) will the official be making or participating in making a contract; (4) does the official have a
financial interest in the contract; (5) does either a remote interest or a non-interest exception apply; (6) does the rule of
necessity apply?

File No. A-17-112
Page No. 5

"Applying this statutory language, we find that the agreement in question
arises directly from the subdivision approval process itself. If a local ordinance
imposes the requirement authorized by section 66485 and the local agency
determines that the supplemental capacity is needed to benefit property not within
the subdivision, the subdivider must provide the supplemental capacity as a
condition for obtaining approval of the subdivision map. Under the agreement, the
subdivider must be reimbursed for the expense of constructing the supplemental
capacity for the benefit of later subdivisions and uses of adjacent property.4

"While subdivision approval may be said to be a 'benefit' received by the
subdivider, the Legislature has determined that such benefit should not bar a
public official 'from subdividing lands owned by him' (§ 1091.1) pursuant to the
Act's requirements and local ordinances adopted thereunder. Even if the
reimbursement could be viewed as a benefit, neither the subdivider nor the local
agency has any choice in the matter; once the local agency orders the
supplemental capacity, it is required by law to provide for reimbursement through
an agreement.

* * *

"As the reimbursement procedure set forth in sections 66485-66489 arises
directly from the subdivision process specified in section 1091.1, is mandated by
statute, and does not involve negotiations that would cause an appearance of
impropriety, we conclude that a county board of supervisors may enter into an
agreement with the subdividers of a parcel of property that would require the
installation of a larger storm drain system than necessary for the particular
subdivision, with the county reimbursing the subdividers for the additional cost,
even though a member of the board of supervisors has an ownership interest in the
parcel. Of course, pursuant to the proviso contained in section 1091.1, the
supervisor would be required to fully disclose the nature of his interest in the
ownership interest in the land to be subdivided and may not cast his vote on any
matter pertaining to the subdivision, including the reimbursement agreement."

This same conclusion applies in this instance. As the reimbursement procedure set forth in
Sections 66485-66489 arises directly from the sub-division process specified in Section 1091.1, is
mandated by statute, and does not involve negotiations that would cause an appearance of
impropriety, we conclude that the City may enter into an agreement and may reimburse for the
additional cost, even though Councilmember Nunley has an ownership interest in the parcel to be
subdivided so long as Councilmember Nunley abstains from any participation on any matter
pertaining to the subdivision, including the reimbursement agreement, and fully discloses the nature
of his interest in the land.

4 [Footnote 2 in original document] "Section 1091.1 contemplates that the subdivision process may involve an
actual contract between the public body and one of its members since it states that the 'member of such board or
commission shall not cast his vote upon any matter or contract concerning said subdivision . . ..'" (Italics added.)

File No. A-17-112
Page No. 6

Section 87100

Your letter requesting advice also referenced advice under Section 87100 of the Political
Reform Act. However, since you did not ask a specific question about Section 87100 and since the
Councilmember would need to abstain from the decisions at issue pursuant to Section 1091.1, we
provide only a general discussion of the application of Section 87100.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and include the
following:

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).) An indirect interest in business or real property means a business
or real property interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of the official.

Your questions presuppose that the decisions concern property that the Councilmember has
an interest in. We assume that his interest in the property to be subdivided is worth $2,000 or more.

Foreseability and Materiality:

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Regulation 18701(a) provides in
pertinent part:

"Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on a financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or
the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest...."

Materiality is defined in Regulation 18702.2. Subdivision (a)(5) provides that the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an
official has a financial interest is material whenever the governmental decision:

"Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or other
land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the parcel or
any variance that changes the permitted use of, or restrictions placed on, that real
property."

A decision to approve or disapprove an application to subdivide property is a land use
entitlement that changes the permitted use of property. Therefore, under the statutes and regulations
of the Act, the Councilmember would have a conflict of interest in the decision. However, the
ultimate consequence of finding that a public official has a conflict of interest under the Act is that
the official may not make, participate in making, or influence that decision and we already have

File No. A-17-112
Page No. 7

concluded that abstention is required under Section 1090. However, Section 87105 of the Act has the additional requirement that an official that holds an office specified in Section 87200 (including as a county supervisor) and has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, the official must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and vote on the item.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel,
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

interest in? 
2. May the Councilmember, as a private developer, properly enter into a subdivision 
agreement with the City regarding lands he owns or has an interest in pursuant to that

of Tulare, may these subdivision agreements contain reimbursement provisions?

CONCLUSION: 1. Pursuant to Section 1091.1, Councilmember Nunley may subdivide property that he owns 
or in which he has an ownership interest, so long as Councilmember Nunley abstains from any 
participation on any matter pertaining to the subdivision, including the reimbursement agreement, 
and fully discloses the nature of his interest in the land.

5. Since we conclude that Councilmember Nunley is permitted to enter into the subdivision

this last question.
